background.

Goals

I am interested developing the means to understand biological molecular networks- both the technologies to accurately measure protein and DNA levels, modifications, and interactions inside the cell, as well as the theoretical foundations necessary to incorporate these and other data into accurate models of dynamic molecular networks.

Where I've been

I left physics in my undergraduate days for the nebulous realm of biophysics. At the time (1989), I was thinking about non-reductionist biological concepts, analogous to macroscopic physical characteristics like heat capacity, that could start to describe the complex behaviors that arise from the interaction of simple parts (so-called "emergent properties"). Of course, at the time I didn't have enough knowledge or inspiration to contribute anything valuable to progress in that pursuit, so I followed a more traditional path and eventually completed a biophysics Ph.D. in the logical endpoint of biological reductionism- structural biology. Along the way I picked up a computer science minor and dabbled in electrical engineering, neurobiology, and the cognitive sciences (as well as a financially crippling addiction to motorcycling).

Where we are

After studying one protein at atomic resolution (using X-ray crystallography) and exploring its place in a specific protein network (through reading the literature and traditional biochemistry and biophysics), it is all the more apparent that deep understanding of many important aspects of biological systems cannot be had with current "post-genomic" technologies. The problem is two-fold. First of all, predicted networks are incomplete and sometimes incorrect. When system behavior is based on the non-linear interaction of many parts, the effects of these errors and omissions are potentially large. The second problem is more fundamental: even given a perfectly wired network, can we understand its dynamic behavior enough to predict reactions to particular perturbations?

What you say!!

For example, drug design reflects our current state of knowledge. For a simply connected pathway overproducing an undesired amount of product (say, cholesterol), the suppression of a critical pathway enzyme (HMG CoA reductase, which catalyzes the committed step in cholestorol biosynthesis) is a perfectly sufficient treatment. However, in a pathway with multiple feedback reactions (e.g. the alpha-factor response pathway in yeast), how does one predict what protein needs to be inhibited to yield a two-fold reduction in some measure of system output (say, transcription of a particular downstream gene) for an identical input (say, external concentration of alpha factor)? A five-fold decrease? An five-fold increase? Can this be done for a generously complete model (a complete parts lists and reasonably accurate pairwise dissociation constants)? A partial model? How complete does the model have to be?

None of these are easy questions, and of course may very well be case dependent. But, a fundamental mission of research here is not modeling this or that system, but essentially learning how to model and simulate systems.

Onwards

While bioinformatics, genomics, and systems biology have been de rigueur topics in faculty research descriptions everywhere recently, only a handful of labs and companies are developing the new tools and theories necessary for bringing about a biological engineering that is truly "predictive" and "intentional". Since September 2000, as a research fellow at TMSI, I have begun to address some of these issues, primarily with work in the wet lab. I have been developing green fluorescent protein-based sensors to measure information flow through the yeast alpha factor mating pheremone response pathway, a model system for signal transduction. These sensors are designed to report, in real-time and at the single-cell level, phosphorylation of pathway intermediates, and the formation and disruption of key complexes, yielding information about their temporal and spatial distributions within the single cell, as well as their cell-to-cell variations. These data will serve as constraints for computer modeling of the pathway taking place in the lab.

Note: Illustrations are reproduced, by permission of the author, from Chris Reynolds's excellent graphic novella "Mauretania" (Penguin Books). Check out the Mauretania Comics page.
